title: Emitting Jobs
description: Fire-and-forget, awaitable, and blocking emission patterns

blocks.text: Hound exposes four emission methods depending on how much you need to know about a job's fate:

rows: Job to be written to storage
All jobs in a chunk to be written atomically (MULTI/EXEC) — all-or-nothing
Job to reach a terminal state

blocks.text: The simplest form. Returns the jobId synchronously and writes to storage in the background:
Because the write is async, the job may not be in storage yet when emit() returns. Use emitAsync() if you need to confirm it was persisted.
Awaits both the state key and queue write before resolving. Use this when you need to confirm the job exists in storage before continuing:

const jobId = await hound.emitAsync('order.process', { orderId: '123' });
console.log('Job persisted:', jobId);

Emit multiple jobs in atomic chunks. Each chunk is a single MULTI/EXEC transaction — every state key write and queue ZADD in the chunk lands in storage, or none do. N jobs cost one round trip per chunk instead of N round trips total:
Each entry accepts the same options as emitAsync(). Jobs from different events or queues can be mixed freely in a single batch.

Atomicity is enforced on every backend: ioredis uses native MULTI/EXEC, Deno KV uses kv.atomic(), and InMemoryStorage snapshots state and rolls back on any failure. If emitBatch() rejects, no jobs in the failed chunk landed — safe to retry, since job IDs are deterministic (same event + data → same id).

Large batches are chunked internally at processorOptions.claimCount (default 200) so a 10k emit fans out into ~50 atomic transactions instead of one unbounded pipeline that would block Redis for other clients.

emitBatch() is available on ctx inside handlers (alongside emit, emitAsync, emitAndWait), on the top-level hound instance, and on the generated HoundClient for typed, cross-runtime batch emission over the HTTP gateway.
Emits a job and waits for it to reach a terminal state (completed or failed). Returns the final JobRecord. Rejects on failure or timeout:
emitAndWait() defaults to a 30-second timeout. Pass timeoutMs to override.
All three emission methods accept an options object as the third argument:

rows: Schedule the job for a future time
Lower number = higher priority within the queue
Cron-only. If false (default), missed ticks are skipped on restart / Reaper recovery; if true, the missed tick is backfilled. Throws if set without repeat.pattern.
